extra.message: User input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec() on line 72 (Python 3 path) and line 76 (Python 2 path). An attacker can submit arbitrary base64-encoded Python code that will be executed on the server, leading to full remote code execution.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: Python 2 fallback path: user input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec(). This is the same code injection vulnerability as line 72 but for Python 2 compatibility.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('p') is concatenated into a file path via os.path.join(base_path, filename) and passed to open() without any validation or sanitization. An attacker can use '../' sequences to traverse directories and read arbitrary files on the server (e.g., ?p=../../../etc/passwd).

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('url') is passed directly to Django's redirect() function without any validation. An attacker can craft a URL like ?url=https://evil.com to redirect users to a malicious site, enabling phishing attacks.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('fwd') is used to look up a function by name via globals().get(forward), and then that function is called with the request. An attacker can invoke any function defined in the views module (e.g., ?fwd=admin) to bypass access controls. This is also a form of code injection since globals() includes all module-level names.

User input from request.GET.get('qs') is rendered in a JavaScript context using {{qs|safe}} in a <script> block. The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript via the qs query parameter (e.g., ?qs=";alert(1);//).

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User-controlled URL path variable is rendered in a JavaScript context using {{path|safe}} in a <script> block. The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript by manipulating the URL path segment.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs') is rendered in an HTML input value attribute using {{qs|safe}}. The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to break out of the attribute and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript (e.g., ?qs="><script>alert(1)</script>).

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('qs') is rendered in a JavaScript context using {{qs|safe}} in a <script> block on the CSRF image page. The |safe filter disables Django's auto-escaping, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The Django CSRF middleware (django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware) is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES. This disables CSRF protection globally for the entire application, making all POST forms vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.

The csrf_image view is decorated with @csrf_exempt, explicitly disabling CSRF protection. Combined with the globally disabled CSRF middleware, this view is fully unprotected against cross-site request forgery.

DEBUG = True is set in the Django settings. When DEBUG is True, Django displays detailed error pages with full stack traces, local variables, and settings values to any user. This exposes sensitive internal application details and should never be enabled in production.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: The Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in settings.py. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, CSRF tokens, and other security-critical functions. A known SECRET_KEY allows attackers to forge session cookies, CSRF tokens, and other signed data.

The XFrameOptionsMiddleware is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES. This middleware sets the X-Frame-Options header to prevent clickjacking attacks. Without it, the application can be embedded in iframes on malicious sites.

cwe: CWE-1021: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames

extra.message: The Django Debug Toolbar's show_toolbar callback allows any user to activate it by passing a 'debug' parameter via GET, POST, or cookies. The debug toolbar exposes SQL queries, settings, request data, and other sensitive internal information to any user who knows to pass the debug parameter.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The missing_access_control view grants admin access based solely on a query parameter (?action=admin) without any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can access the admin interface by simply adding ?action=admin to the URL.

The dor_user_profile view allows any user to view and modify any other user's profile by simply changing the userid parameter in the URL (e.g., /direct-object-references/users/2). There is no authentication or authorization check to verify the requesting user owns the profile being accessed or modified.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The boom view intentionally raises an unhandled exception. Combined with DEBUG=True, this exposes full stack traces, local variables, Django settings (including SECRET_KEY), and environment information to any user who visits this URL.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
